primary_diagnosis.name: Uncomplicated acute middle-ear infection (mild), left ear

comorbidities.name: Recent viral upper respiratory tract infection

pathophysiology_summary: A preceding viral upper respiratory tract infection causes Eustachian tube dysfunction and mucosal oedema in a young child (short, horizontal Eustachian tube). Impaired middle-ear ventilation and drainage lead to a middle-ear effusion that becomes secondarily inflamed, producing a bulging, erythematous tympanic membrane, reduced tympanic mobility, conductive hearing loss and acute severe otalgia. In a well, consolable, feeding child with no post-auricular signs and no meningism, this is uncomplicated and frequently viral or self-limiting; the evidence-based approach for mild disease is adequate analgesia with watchful waiting (delayed antibiotic prescription) rather than immediate antibiotics. The clinical value of assessment is to confirm the diagnosis on otoscopy and to actively exclude the important complications — acute mastoiditis, intracranial spread/meningitis, and tympanic membrane perforation.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden severe unilateral otalgia with ear-tugging and fever in a young child a few days after a cold is the classic AOM presentation.
Can cause unilateral ear pain; must be distinguished by pinna/tragal tenderness and canal appearance.
A serious complication of AOM to actively exclude — post-auricular swelling/redness, tenderness, protruding auricle.
Ear pain can be referred; less likely with local ear signs but considered before examination.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on, AOM should lead but the AI must actively hold mastoiditis and intracranial spread as important-not-to-miss and screen for them, without leaping to antibiotics or emergency escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: URTI prodrome, unilateral otalgia, reduced hearing, no pinna tenderness; complications screened negative.
No pain on pinna/tragal movement and clear canal argue strongly against otitis externa.
No post-auricular swelling/redness/tenderness and auricle not displaced — mastoiditis screened negative.
No meningism, alert and consolable, no non-blanching rash — actively excluded but must remain a safety-net trigger.

differential_progression.scoring_note: With complications screened negative and the child systemically well, the picture is uncomplicated mild AOM. The correct action is analgesia + watchful waiting + in-person otoscopy review + safety-net — NOT ED/000 and NOT immediate antibiotics.

differential.evidence_basis: Presumptive uncomplicated mild AOM pending otoscopic confirmation; manage symptomatically with delayed-prescription safety-net.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot visualise the drum, so the final position is presumptive AOM pending in-person otoscopy. Confident definitive diagnosis, or immediate antibiotics, or emergency escalation are all incorrect for this well child.

red_flags.description: Post-auricular redness, swelling or tenderness, or an anteriorly displaced/protruding auricle — acute mastoiditis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Mastoiditis is the principal suppurative complication of AOM; it requires urgent in-person assessment, IV antibiotics and possible surgical drainage. Screened negative here.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undiagnosed mastoiditis can progress to subperiosteal abscess, facial nerve palsy or intracranial spread.

red_flags.description: Meningism — neck stiffness, marked drowsiness/irritability, non-blanching rash, persistent vomiting — intracranial spread/meningitis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Intracranial complications of otitis media are life-threatening and time-critical. Screened negative — child alert, consolable, no meningism or rash.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed bacterial meningitis carries high mortality and neurological morbidity; delay to antibiotics worsens outcome.

red_flags.description: Sudden discharge of fluid, pus or blood from the ear canal — tympanic membrane perforation
red_flags.clinical_significance: Perforation usually relieves pressure and pain and generally heals, but warrants review to confirm and guide ear-canal care. Absent at presentation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised persistent perforation or chronic suppurative otitis media if not reviewed.

red_flags.description: Refusing all fluids, reduced wet nappies, floppy/lethargic or inconsolable beyond the pain — dehydration or systemic sepsis in a young child
red_flags.clinical_significance: A young child who stops drinking or becomes systemically unwell needs urgent in-person assessment. Screened negative — feeding, wet nappies, consolable.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive dehydration or unrecognised serious bacterial infection.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reaching for immediate antibiotics for mild AOM in a well child rather than analgesia with watchful waiting — the key stewardship error this case probes.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triage of a common paediatric complaint
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Escalating a well, consolable, feeding febrile toddler to ED or 000 because the pain is severe, when analgesia plus in-person GP review and safety-netting is correct.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Premature closure on the ear, missing mastoiditis/meningitis screen
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on 'ear infection' and stopping before actively screening for the post-auricular and meningism red flags.
Stating a definitive middle-ear diagnosis when the tympanic membrane cannot be visualised remotely — the diagnosis is presumptive pending otoscopy.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Otoscopy — direct visualisation of the tympanic membrane (bulging, erythema, loss of light reflex) that confirms acute otitis media
Pneumatic otoscopy / tympanometry — reduced tympanic membrane mobility and middle-ear effusion
Examination of the external canal and pinna, and palpation of the post-auricular region and cervical lymph nodes
Auscultation of the chest and clinician-measured vital signs (heart rate, respiratory rate, blood pressure, oxygen saturation)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Advise regular weight-based analgesia now and arrange in-person GP (or after-hours GP) review for otoscopy within 24-48 hours to confirm the diagnosis and exclude complications; give explicit red-flag safety-netting for same-day/emergency escalation. Do not default to ED or 000 for this well, consolable, feeding child.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Confidently diagnosing and treating a middle-ear infection without otoscopy risks both missing an alternative/serious cause and unnecessary antibiotic exposure; over-escalating to ED/000 causes avoidable emergency presentation and family distress.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Acute otitis media (eTG, current)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: For most children with acute otitis media, analgesia and watchful waiting (delayed/deferred antibiotic prescription) is appropriate; immediate antibiotics are reserved for specific higher-risk groups and severe or complicated disease.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RCH Melbourne Clinical Practice Guideline: Acute otitis media
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Adequate analgesia is the mainstay; review and safety-net for mastoiditis and systemic features; antibiotics if not improving at 48 hours or if red flags develop.
